course_title: Introduction to Philosophy: What is Philosophy?
course_description:
The questions of what philosophy is and what philosophers do are approached through a discussion of the work of philosophers such as Descartes, Locke, Hume, Sartre, Fanon, Beauvoir, and Merleau-Ponty. The first section of the course is devoted to questions of what there is (metaphysics) and what it is to know (epistemology), and topics covered include scepticism, the relationship between mind and body, and the problem of personal identity. The second section responds to earlier work on Descartes by providing an introduction to philosophy from the existential and phenomenological perspectives of embodiment, eroticism, inter-subjectivity, and love. It explores ideas of intentionality and the lived body, the ontological significance of the gaze, relations between the self and the other, and the relevance of these for a philosophical understanding of our everyday lives. Note: This course is available online, as well as face-to-face in Semester 1.

The questions of what philosophy is and what philosophers do are approached through a discussion of the work of philosophers such as Plato, Boethius, Aquinas, Descartes, Camus, Sartre, andﾠBeauvoir. The first section of the course is devoted to questions of the basis what it means to 'do' philosophy, the place of beauty and love in existence, arguments around the existence of God, how we come to 'know' anything, and what the nature of reality is, ranging accross the history of philosophy from the Classical to the Early Modern periods. The second section provides an introduction to philosophy from the existential and phenomenological perspectives of the absurd and meaning, existentialistﾠethics, Indigenous existentialism, authenticity and freedom, politics and the Other.

course_aims: Philosophy uses critical thinking and logical argument to look at questions relating to the meaning of life, for example, the nature of the external world, the nature of human beings, knowledge, meaning, freedom, ethics and politics. This course introduces students to some of the basic questions of philosophy in the context of reading some of the major thinkers of modern philosophy, including Descartes, Locke, Hume, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Fanon and Beauvoir. Students in this course are given an opportunity to develop their own ideas and their own voice and to understand the role of philosophical thinking in everyday life.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand some of the central debates in philosophy (breadth and depth)
Demonstrate basic skills of effective thinking and communication (clarity, relevance, accuracy)
Conduct independent philosophical research and inquiry
Identify, analyse and evaluate arguments or points of view
Understand the role of philosophy in everyday life

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Students discuss the set reading for the relevant week. Marks are credited for participation, which includes demonstration of reading the set text and contribution to a community of philosophical inquiry (see marking and criteria). Tutorials run during weeks 2-12 (with no tutorial in week 7) and are face-to-face. This is an in-person assessment not permitting use of AI. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Critical reading task
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be presented with a case study or hypothetical based on the philosophers of weeks 2-4 (Plato, Boethius, Aquinas). Students are required to write a 300 word analysis of the case through the lens of one of the chosen philosophers, then evaluate using their own individual judgement and perspective. Further details are available on the Blackboard site under 'Assessment'. Assignments must be submitted via TurnItIn (on Blackboard); check that your submission has been received. AI is not to be used for writing or planning this Assessment. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

assessment_details.title: Essay 1
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be required to write a philosophical essay on a question from Part One of the course. Questions will be specified in the 'Essay 1 Task Sheet' on the Blackboard site under 'Assessment'. Word count (including discursive footnotes but excluding references and bibliography): 1100-1200 words. For this assignment you may use any recognised referencing style. Assignments must be submitted via TurnItIn (on Blackboard); check that your submission has been received. AI generation is not to be used for this Assessment. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: 10 x one hour tutorials Choose one tutorial time from the options on Allocate+. Tutorials will be held weeks 2-8, 10-12. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Introduction: What is Philosophy? Classical Philosophy - Plato's Apology & Introduction to the Course Learning outcomes: L01, L05
Philosophy and Love Classical Philosophy - Plato's Symposium Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Life Medieval Philosophy - Boethius' Consolation of Philosophy Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and God Medieval Philosophy - Aquinas' Summa Theologica Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Truth Early Modern Philosophy - Descartes' Meditations I & II Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Philosophy and Reality Early Modern Philosophy - Descartes' Meditations V & IV Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05

Philosophy and Suspicion Modern Philosophy - Marx, Nietzsche, and Freud This lecture will also include a workshop on writing argumentative essays in philosophy, in preparation for the first major assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Albert Camus: The absurd and meaning Albert Camus, Selections from the Myth of Sisyphus. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Albert Camus: The absurd and meaning Albert Camus, Selections from the Myth of Sisyphus Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Jean-Paul Sartre: Existentialism and humanism Jean-Paul Sartre, Existentialism is a Humanism. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Brendan Hokowhitu: Indigenous Existentialism Brendan Hokowhitu: Indigenous Existentialism and the body. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Simone de Beauvoir: Authenticity and freedom Simone de Beauvoir,Pyrrhus and Cineas Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
Simone de Beauvoir: Politics and the Other Simone de Beauvoir, Pyrrhus and Cineas. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05
